subject: Physics
q: Let a total charge <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></math> be distributed in a sphere of radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> with the charge density given by <math><mi>ρ</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> is the distance from the centre. Two charges <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> , of <math><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></math> each, are placed on diametrically opposite points, at equal distance, a, from the centre. If <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> do not experience any force, then:
question: Let a total charge <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></math> be distributed in a sphere of radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> with the charge density given by <math><mi>ρ</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> is the distance from the centre. Two charges <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> , of <math><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></math> each, are placed on diametrically opposite points, at equal distance, a, from the centre. If <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> do not experience any force, then:
solution:
As total charge on sphere is =2Q
2Q=∫0Rkr4πr2dr⇒kπr4=2Q
Charged enclosed in the sphere of radius  a 
q=∫0akr×4πr2dr=kπa4
As force on  Q  is zero
14πε0×Q22a2=kπa44πε0×a2×Q
⇒Q24=kπa4Q
=a42QR4Q
R4=8a4⇒R=814 a or  a=8-14R

subject: Physics
q: Two spherical conductors $A$ and $B$ of radii $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and $2 \mathrm{~mm}$ are separated by a distance of $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and are uniformly charged. If the spheres are connected by a conducting wire then in equilibrium condition, the ratio of the magnitude of the electric fields at the surface of spheres $A$ and $B$ is
question: Two spherical conductors $A$ and $B$ of radii $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and $2 \mathrm{~mm}$ are separated by a distance of $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and are uniformly charged. If the spheres are connected by a conducting wire then in equilibrium condition, the ratio of the magnitude of the electric fields at the surface of spheres $A$ and $B$ is
solution: $\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{2}{1}$

subject: Physics
q:
As shown in figure, a cuboid lies in a region with electric field E=2x2i^-4yj^+6k^ N C-1. The magnitude of charge within the cuboid is nε0 C.

The value of n is ______ (if dimension of cuboid is 1×2×3 m3)
question:
As shown in figure, a cuboid lies in a region with electric field E=2x2i^-4yj^+6k^ N C-1. The magnitude of charge within the cuboid is nε0 C.

The value of n is ______ (if dimension of cuboid is 1×2×3 m3)
solution:
Given:

E→=2x2i^-4yj^+6k^

For top and bottom faces, as value of x=0 & y=0, electric field becomes constant and therefore net flux through these two faces will be zero(ϕz=0).

Now, Ex=2x2, Ey=-4y and Ez=6.

Change in net flux through face at x=1 and at x=0 parallel to y-z plane can written as, ϕx=2×12×2×3-0=12.

Similarly, net flux through face at y=2 and y=0 parallel to x-z plane can be written as, ϕy=-4×2×1×3-0=-24

ϕnet=12-24=-12

Using Gauss's law,

ϕnet=qε0

⇒q=12ε0

subject: Physics
q: Find out the surface charge density at the intersection of point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> plane and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis, in the region of uniform line charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nC</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> lying along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math>-axis in free space.
question: Find out the surface charge density at the intersection of point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> plane and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis, in the region of uniform line charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nC</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> lying along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math>-axis in free space.
solution:
2 Kλr=σε0  x=3 m

⇒σ=0.424×10-9 C m-2

subject: Physics
q: The total charge enclosed in an incremental volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup></math> located at the origin is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nC</mi><mo>,</mo></math> if electric flux density of its field is found as<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic">-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mi>y</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic">-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mi>cosy</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>
question: The total charge enclosed in an incremental volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup></math> located at the origin is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nC</mi><mo>,</mo></math> if electric flux density of its field is found as<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic">-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mi>y</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic">-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mi>cosy</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>
solution:
Electric flux density,

D→= charge  Area ×r^=Q4πr2r^=ϵ0Q4πϵ0r2r^

⇒E→=D→ϵ0=e-xsinyi^-e-xcos(j^+2zk)^ϵ0

Also, by Gauss's law,

ρϵ0=∂∂xi^+∂∂yj^+∂∂zk^·E→=∂∂xi^+∂∂yj^+∂∂zk^·D→ϵ0

⇒ρ=∂∂xe-xsiny+∂∂y-e-xcosy+∂∂z(2z)

ρ=-e-xsiny+e-xsiny+2
At origin ρ=-e-0sin0+e-0sin0+2
ρ=2 C m-3

Charge =ρ× volume =2×2×10-9=4×10-9=
4 nC

subject: Physics
q: An electric dipole of mass m, charge q, and length \(l\) is placed in a uniform electric field \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\mathrm{E}_0 \hat{i}\). When the dipole is rotated slightly from its equilibrium position and released, the time period of its oscillations will be:
question: An electric dipole of mass m, charge q, and length \(l\) is placed in a uniform electric field \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\mathrm{E}_0 \hat{i}\). When the dipole is rotated slightly from its equilibrium position and released, the time period of its oscillations will be:
solution: $\tau=P E_0 \sin \theta$ If $\theta$ is small $\begin{aligned} \tau & =-\left(P E_0\right) \theta \\ I & =m\left(\frac{l}{2}\right)^2 \cdot 2=\frac{m l^2}{2} \\ T & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m l^2}{2 \cdot P E_0}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m l^2}{2 \cdot q / E_0}} \\ T & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m l}{2 q E_0}} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A thin infinite sheet charge and an infinite line charge of respective charge densities +σ and +λ are placed parallel at 5 m distance from each other. Points P and Q are at 3π m and 4π m perpendicular distances from line charge towards sheet charge, respectively. Ep and Eq are the magnitudes of resultant electric field intensities at point P and Q respectively. If EPEQ=4a for 2|σ|=|λ|, then the value of a is _____.
question: A thin infinite sheet charge and an infinite line charge of respective charge densities +σ and +λ are placed parallel at 5 m distance from each other. Points P and Q are at 3π m and 4π m perpendicular distances from line charge towards sheet charge, respectively. Ep and Eq are the magnitudes of resultant electric field intensities at point P and Q respectively. If EPEQ=4a for 2|σ|=|λ|, then the value of a is _____.
solution:
Let's consider the following diagram:

The formula to calculate the electric field due to an infinitely long charged sheet is given by

Es=σ2ε0   ...1

The formula to calculate the electric field due to an infinitely long line charge is given by

El=λ2πε0r   ...2

Using equations (1) and (2), the electric field at P can be calculated as follows:

EP=σ2ε0-14πε02λ3π=σ2ε0-λ6ε0=σ6ε0   ...3

Similarly, the electric field at Q is given by

EQ=σ2ε0-14πε02λ4π=σ2ε0-λ8ε0=σ4ε0   ...4

Divide equation (3) by equation (4) to obtain the required ratio.

EPEQ=σ6ε0σ4ε0=46

Hence, a=6.

subject: Physics
q: A Point dipole <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> is kept at the origin. The potential and electric field due to this dipole on the <math><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> at a distance <math><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></math> are, respectively: (Taken <math><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at infinity)
question: A Point dipole <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> is kept at the origin. The potential and electric field due to this dipole on the <math><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> at a distance <math><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></math> are, respectively: (Taken <math><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at infinity)
solution:
E→=-p→4πε0r3=-p→4πε0d3
V=0

subject: Physics
q: If a charge q is placed at the centre of a closed hemispherical non-conducting surface, the total flux passing through the flat surface would be
question: If a charge q is placed at the centre of a closed hemispherical non-conducting surface, the total flux passing through the flat surface would be
solution:
Assume a complete sphere, then flux due to a charge placed at the center of sphere is ϕ=qε0

Flux through hemisphere surface is equal to flux passing through flat surface ϕ=12×qε0=q2ε0

subject: Physics
q: A long cylindrical volume contains a uniformly distributed charge of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math>. The radius of cylindrical volume is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. A charge particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>q</mi></mfenced></math> revolves around the cylinder in a circular path. The kinetic energy of the particle is :
question: A long cylindrical volume contains a uniformly distributed charge of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math>. The radius of cylindrical volume is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. A charge particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>q</mi></mfenced></math> revolves around the cylinder in a circular path. The kinetic energy of the particle is :
solution:
Electric field due to a long cylinder is,

E=λ2πε0r=ρA2πε0r

If a charge q is rotating in a circle of radius r

mv2r=qE  ⇒mv2r=qρA2πε0r⇒12mv2=qρπR24πε0=qρR24ε0

subject: Physics
q: Determine the electric dipole moment of the system of three charges, placed on the vertices of an equilateral triangle, as shown in the figure:
question: Determine the electric dipole moment of the system of three charges, placed on the vertices of an equilateral triangle, as shown in the figure:
solution:
P→1=ql

P→2=ql



Angle between them 60o

∴ Resultant dipole moment P=ql2+ql2+2ql2cos⁡60o

=3 ql

As P→1=P→2 direction of resultant is along negative y axis thus option is -3 ql j^

subject: Physics
q: The region between two concentric spheres of radii 'a' and 'b', respectively (see figure), has volume charge density ρ=Ar , where A is a constant and r is the distance from the centre. At the centre of the spheres is a point charge Q. The value of A such that the electric field in the region between the spheres will be constant, is:
question: The region between two concentric spheres of radii 'a' and 'b', respectively (see figure), has volume charge density ρ=Ar , where A is a constant and r is the distance from the centre. At the centre of the spheres is a point charge Q. The value of A such that the electric field in the region between the spheres will be constant, is:
solution:
E=E1+E2

⇒E=KQ+∫ar4πx2dxAxr2

E=KQr2+4πAr22r2-a22r2

dEdr=0

⇒-2Qr3+8πAa22r3=0

A=Q2πa2

subject: Physics
q: Two infinite identical charged sheets and a charged spherical body of charge density ' $\rho$ ' are arranged as shown in figure. Then the correct relation between the electrical fields at $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D points is :
question: Two infinite identical charged sheets and a charged spherical body of charge density ' $\rho$ ' are arranged as shown in figure. Then the correct relation between the electrical fields at $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D points is :
solution:
Conceptual
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{C}} \neq \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{D}} \\ & \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}} \gt \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A negative test charge is moving near a long straight wire carrying a current. The force acting on the test charge is parallel to the direction of the current. The motion of the charge is:
question: A negative test charge is moving near a long straight wire carrying a current. The force acting on the test charge is parallel to the direction of the current. The motion of the charge is:
options: Parallel to the wire along the current
Parallel to the wire opposite to the current
solution:
Force on a moving charge, F→=qv→×B→

Here, ∵q=-ve 

⇒F→=-qv→×B→

∴motion of-q is towards the wire.

subject: Physics
q: A solid conducting sphere, having a charge <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is surrounded by an uncharged conducting hollow spherical shell. Let the potential difference between the surface of the solid sphere and that of the outer surface of the hollow shell be <math><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the shell is now given a charge of <math><mo>–</mo><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the new potential difference between the same two surfaces is:
question: A solid conducting sphere, having a charge <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is surrounded by an uncharged conducting hollow spherical shell. Let the potential difference between the surface of the solid sphere and that of the outer surface of the hollow shell be <math><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the shell is now given a charge of <math><mo>–</mo><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the new potential difference between the same two surfaces is:
solution:
First condition:

Both conducting spheres are shown.
Vin-Vout=KQr1-KQr2
⇒V=KQ1r1-1r2
Second condition:

Shell is now given charge -4Q .
Vin-Vout=KQr1-4KQr2-KQr2-4KQr2
=KQr1-KQr2
=KQ1r1-1r2=V

subject: Physics
q: An electron revolves around an infinite cylindrical wire having uniform linear charge density 2×10-8 C m-1 in circular path under the influence of attractive electrostatic field as shown in the figure. The velocity of electron with which it is revolving is ______________ ×106 m s-1. Given mass of electron =9×10-31 kg
question: An electron revolves around an infinite cylindrical wire having uniform linear charge density 2×10-8 C m-1 in circular path under the influence of attractive electrostatic field as shown in the figure. The velocity of electron with which it is revolving is ______________ ×106 m s-1. Given mass of electron =9×10-31 kg
solution:
The electric field is given by 

E=2kλr

The required centripetal force is 

F=mv2r

The force due to the field is equal to the required centripetal force,

⇒2kλre=mv2r

⇒v=2kλem=2×9×109×2×10-8×1.6×10-199×10-31

=1.6×4×1013=4×2×106 m s-1

= 8 × 106 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: If the net electric field at point $\mathrm{P}$ along $\mathrm{Y}$ axis is zero, then the ratio of $\left|\frac{q_2}{q_3}\right|$ is $\frac{8}{5 \sqrt{x}}$, where $x=$ _____.
question: If the net electric field at point $\mathrm{P}$ along $\mathrm{Y}$ axis is zero, then the ratio of $\left|\frac{q_2}{q_3}\right|$ is $\frac{8}{5 \sqrt{x}}$, where $x=$ _____.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{Kq}_2}{20} \cos \beta=\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_3}{25} \cos \theta \\ & \frac{\mathrm{Kq}_2}{20} \frac{4}{\sqrt{20}}=\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_3}{25} \frac{4}{\sqrt{25}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{q}_2}{\mathrm{q}_3}=\frac{20}{25} \sqrt{\frac{20}{25}}=\frac{8}{5 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{8 \times 25 \sqrt{25}}{5 \times 20 \sqrt{20}} \\ & \mathrm{x}=5\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is to be divided into two. The distance between the two divided charges is constant. The magnitude of the divided charges so that the force between them is maximum, will be:
question: A charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is to be divided into two. The distance between the two divided charges is constant. The magnitude of the divided charges so that the force between them is maximum, will be:
solution:
If after division, one of the charge is q the other charge will be 4-q. The force between them can be written as,

F=Kq4-qd2

For the force between them to be maximum,

dFdq=0⇒Kd24-2q=0

⇒q=2 μC

subject: Physics
q: Two charged conducting spheres of radii $a$ and $b$ are connected to each other by a conducting wire. The ratio of charges of the two spheres respectively is:
question: Two charged conducting spheres of radii $a$ and $b$ are connected to each other by a conducting wire. The ratio of charges of the two spheres respectively is:
solution:
Potential at surface will be same
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{Kq}_1}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_2}{\mathrm{~b}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{q}_1}{\mathrm{q}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: On moving a charge of 20 coulombs by $2 \mathrm{~cm}, 2 \mathrm{~J}$ of work is done, then the potential difference between the points is
question: On moving a charge of 20 coulombs by $2 \mathrm{~cm}, 2 \mathrm{~J}$ of work is done, then the potential difference between the points is
solution:
$$
\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{qV} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=2 / 20=0.1 \mathrm{~V}
$$
Here $W$ is the work done in moving charge $q$ from point $A$ to $B$

subject: Physics
q: Consider a circular loop that is uniformly charged and has a radius $\mathrm{a} \sqrt{2}$. Find the position along the positive z -axis of the cartesian coordinate system where the electric field is maximum if the ring was assumed to be placed in xy-plane at the origin :
question: Consider a circular loop that is uniformly charged and has a radius $\mathrm{a} \sqrt{2}$. Find the position along the positive z -axis of the cartesian coordinate system where the electric field is maximum if the ring was assumed to be placed in xy-plane at the origin :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{KQr}}{\left(\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{R}^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{a}}{\sqrt{2}}=\mathrm{a}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The electric potential $V(x)$ in a region around the origin is given by $V(x)=4 x^2$ volts. The electric charge enclosed in a cube of $1 \mathrm{~m}$ side with its centre at the origin is (in coulomb)
question: The electric potential $V(x)$ in a region around the origin is given by $V(x)=4 x^2$ volts. The electric charge enclosed in a cube of $1 \mathrm{~m}$ side with its centre at the origin is (in coulomb)
solution: Charges reside only on the outer surface of a conductor with cavity.

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> each are placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> apart. A third point charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from mid-point on the perpendicular bisector. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> at which charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> will experience the maximum Coulomb's force is:
question: Two point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> each are placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> apart. A third point charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from mid-point on the perpendicular bisector. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> at which charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> will experience the maximum Coulomb's force is:
solution:
The distance between q and Q is r=x2+d22=x2+d24.

The force between q and Q will be F=kqQr2.

From the given figure we can see that only vertical components of the forces will add, and the horizontal components will get cancelled out. Therefore,

Fnet=2kqQr2cosθ=2kqQr2×xr=kqQxx2+d2432

For force to be maximum, dFnetdx=0.

⇒kqQx2+d2432-x×32x2+d2412×2xx2+d24322=0

⇒x2+d24=3x2⇒2x2=d24⇒x=d22

subject: Physics
q:
Experimentally it is found that 12.8 eV energy is required to separate a hydrogen atom into a proton and an electron. So the orbital radius of the electron in a hydrogen atom is 9x×10-10 m.

The value of the x is: _____.

(1 eV=1.6×10-19 J, 14πε0=9×109 Nm2C2 and electronic charge =1.6×10-19 C)
question:
Experimentally it is found that 12.8 eV energy is required to separate a hydrogen atom into a proton and an electron. So the orbital radius of the electron in a hydrogen atom is 9x×10-10 m.

The value of the x is: _____.

(1 eV=1.6×10-19 J, 14πε0=9×109 Nm2C2 and electronic charge =1.6×10-19 C)
solution:
The given data is 

q=1.6×10-19 C14πε0=9×109 N m2 C-2

The formula for the value of energy in a hydrogen atom is given by 

E=q28πε0r⇒E=q22(4πε0)r

⇒r=9×109×1.6×10-1912.8×2

⇒r=916×10-10 m

It is given that r=9x×10-10 m⇒916×10-10=9x×10-10 ⇒x=16

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass ' $m$ ' and charge ' $q$ ' is fastened to one end ' $A$ ' of a massless string having equilibrium length $l$, whose other end is fixed at point ' $O$ '. The whole system is placed on a frictionless horizontal plane and is initially at rest. If uniform electric field is switched on along the direction as shown in figure, then the speed of the particle when it crosses the x -axis is
question: A particle of mass ' $m$ ' and charge ' $q$ ' is fastened to one end ' $A$ ' of a massless string having equilibrium length $l$, whose other end is fixed at point ' $O$ '. The whole system is placed on a frictionless horizontal plane and is initially at rest. If uniform electric field is switched on along the direction as shown in figure, then the speed of the particle when it crosses the x -axis is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{all}}=\Delta \mathrm{k} \\ & \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}} \\ & \mathrm{qE} \frac{\ell}{2}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2-0 \\ & \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{qE} \ell}{\mathrm{m}}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: An electric dipole of dipole moment is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> placed in a uniform electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in such a way that dipole moment is along electric field. The work done in rotating dipole by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo>°</mo><mo> </mo></math>in this field will be_______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mJ</mi></math>.
question: An electric dipole of dipole moment is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> placed in a uniform electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in such a way that dipole moment is along electric field. The work done in rotating dipole by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo>°</mo><mo> </mo></math>in this field will be_______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mJ</mi></math>.
solution:
The work done for rotating a dipole is given by the change in the potential energy. The potential energy is given by

U=-p→·E→

W =∆U = Uf−Ui

So, the work done is 

 W=-pEcos180°-(-pEcos0°)=2pE=2×6×10-6×1.5×103=18 mJ

q: An electric charge $10^{-3} \mu \mathrm{C}$ is placed at the origin $(0,0)$ of $X-Y$ co-ordinate system. Two points $A$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are situated at $(\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2})$ and $(2,0)$ respectively. The potential difference between the points $A$ and $B$ will be
question: An electric charge $10^{-3} \mu \mathrm{C}$ is placed at the origin $(0,0)$ of $X-Y$ co-ordinate system. Two points $A$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are situated at $(\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2})$ and $(2,0)$ respectively. The potential difference between the points $A$ and $B$ will be
solution: Both points are at same distance from the charge.

subject: Physics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is the gravitational potential due to sphere of uniform density on its surface, then its value at the centre of sphere will be:
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is the gravitational potential due to sphere of uniform density on its surface, then its value at the centre of sphere will be:
solution:
The magnitude of gravitational potential of a solid sphere at a distance r from the centre is 

V=-GM2R3(3R2-r2)

At r=R, V=-GMR.

At r=0, V'=-GM2R3(3R2)=-3GM2R

Clearly, V'=3V2.

subject: Physics
q: Three concentric metal shells A, B and C of respective radii a, b and c (a < b < c) have surface charge densities  +σ, -σ  and +σ respectively. The potential of shell B  is:
question: Three concentric metal shells A, B and C of respective radii a, b and c (a < b < c) have surface charge densities  +σ, -σ  and +σ respectively. The potential of shell B  is:
solution:
Net potential at B is due to the superposition of potential due to all shells, 

∨B=∨BI+∨BII+∨BIII

=K4πa2σb+K4πb2-σb+K4πc2σc

Required potential of b

=σa2ε0b-σb2ε0b+σc2ε0c

=σε0a2-b2b+c

Find the electric field intensity along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math>-axis at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> from origin:
question: A uniformly charged disc of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> having surface charge density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math> is placed in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></math> plane with its center at the origin. Find the electric field intensity along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math>-axis at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> from origin:
solution: E=∫0R14πε0×σ×2πxdx×xx2+Z232=σ2ε01-ZZ2+R21/2

subject: Physics
q: A dipole comprises of two charged particles of identical magnitude q and opposite in nature. The mass m of the positive charged particle is half of the mass of the negative charged particle. The two charges are separated by a distance l . If the dipole is placed in a uniform electric field E→; in such a way that dipole axis makes a very small angle with the electric field, E→. The angular frequency of the oscillations of the dipole when released is given by:
question: A dipole comprises of two charged particles of identical magnitude q and opposite in nature. The mass m of the positive charged particle is half of the mass of the negative charged particle. The two charges are separated by a distance l . If the dipole is placed in a uniform electric field E→; in such a way that dipole axis makes a very small angle with the electric field, E→. The angular frequency of the oscillations of the dipole when released is given by:
solution:
The data given is 

l=distance between the charges

q=charge

m=mass of the positive charge

2m=mass of negative charge 

The moment of inertia of the system is 

I=m×2mm+2ml2=2ml23

The angular frequency can be written as 

ω=pEI   ...(i)

Substituting the value of moment of inertia in the equation (i) we get 

ω=pE2ml23=3pE2ml2

The value of the dipole moment is

p=ql

Hence, the angular frequency becomes

ω=3qE2ml

This question was given bonus by NTA as none of the options matched in the original paper.

subject: Physics
q: A particle of charge $q$ and mass $m$ is projected from origin with an initial velocity $\vec{v} = \left(\dfrac{v_0}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{x} + \dfrac{v_0}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{y}\right)$. There exists a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B} = B_0 \hat{z}$ and a space varying electric field $\vec{E} = E_0 e^{-\lambda x}\hat{x}$ within the region $0 \leq x \leq L$. After travelling a distance such that $x$-coordinate has changed from $x=0$ to $x=L$, the change in the kinetic energy is _______.
question: A particle of charge $q$ and mass $m$ is projected from origin with an initial velocity $\vec{v} = \left(\dfrac{v_0}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{x} + \dfrac{v_0}{\sqrt{2}}\hat{y}\right)$. There exists a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B} = B_0 \hat{z}$ and a space varying electric field $\vec{E} = E_0 e^{-\lambda x}\hat{x}$ within the region $0 \leq x \leq L$. After travelling a distance such that $x$-coordinate has changed from $x=0$ to $x=L$, the change in the kinetic energy is _______.
solution:
According to the work-energy theorem, the change in kinetic energy of a particle is equal to the total work done by all the forces acting on it.

The forces acting on the particle are the electric force and the magnetic force. The magnetic force is given by $\vec{F}_B = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$. Since the magnetic force is always perpendicular to the velocity vector of the particle, the work done by the magnetic field is zero.

The change in kinetic energy is equal to the work done by the electric field alone. The electric force is $\vec{F}_E = q\vec{E} = qE_0 e^{-\lambda x}\hat{x}$.

The work done by the electric field as the particle moves from $x = 0$ to $x = L$ is given by

$W_E = \int_{0}^{L} \vec{F}_E \cdot d\vec{r} = \int_{0}^{L} qE_0 e^{-\lambda x} dx$

Evaluating the integral, we get

$W_E = qE_0 \left[ \dfrac{e^{-\lambda x}}{-\lambda} \right]_{0}^{L}$

$W_E = -\dfrac{qE_0}{\lambda} (e^{-\lambda L} - e^{0})$

$W_E = \dfrac{qE_0}{\lambda} [1 - e^{-\lambda L}]$

Thus, the change in kinetic energy is $\dfrac{qE_0}{\lambda} [1 - e^{-\lambda L}]$.

Answer: $\dfrac{qE_0}{\lambda}[1 - e^{-\lambda L}]$

subject: Physics
q:
Consider two identical metallic spheres of radius $R$ each having charge $Q$ and mass $m$. Their centers have an initial separation of $4 R$. Both the spheres are given an initial speed of $u$ towards each other. The minimum value of $u$, so that they can just touch each other is :
(Take $k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}$ and assume $k Q^{2}>G m^{2}$ where $G$ is the Gravitational constant)
question:
Consider two identical metallic spheres of radius $R$ each having charge $Q$ and mass $m$. Their centers have an initial separation of $4 R$. Both the spheres are given an initial speed of $u$ towards each other. The minimum value of $u$, so that they can just touch each other is :
(Take $k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}$ and assume $k Q^{2}>G m^{2}$ where $G$ is the Gravitational constant)
options: $\sqrt{\frac{k Q^{2}}{2 m R}\left(1-\frac{G m^{2}}{2 k Q^{2}}\right)}$
solution:
Using energy conservation, the system's kinetic energy must overcome the increase in electrostatic potential energy as the spheres approach.
Initial state: separation 4R, speed u each.
Final state: touching (separation 2R), speed zero.
Change in potential energy: $\Delta U = k\frac{Q^2}{2R} - k\frac{Q^2}{4R} = k\frac{Q^2}{4R}$.
Initial kinetic energy: $KE_i = mu^2$.
Energy equation: $mu^2 = k\frac{Q^2}{4R}\left(1 - \frac{Gm^2}{kQ^2}\right)$.
Therefore, $u = \sqrt{\frac{kQ^2}{4mR}\left(1 - \frac{Gm^2}{kQ^2}\right)}$.

subject: Physics
q:
The electric potential at the surface of an atomic nucleus (Z=50) of radius 9×10-13 cm is α×106 V. What is the value of α?

(Charge of proton 1.6×10-19 C)
question:
The electric potential at the surface of an atomic nucleus (Z=50) of radius 9×10-13 cm is α×106 V. What is the value of α?

(Charge of proton 1.6×10-19 C)
solution:
The electric potential at the surface of an atomic nucleus can be written as

V=kQR=k·ZeR   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

V=9×109×50×1.6×10-199×10-13×10-2 V=8×106 V

Hence, α=8.
